erroneous_event: He messages her anyway, careful and earnest: <error>[I hope everything is okay. If the therapist with the newer implant still can't stabilize things, is there anything I can do to help? Please let me know.]</error> She replies at once but dodges the truth.
story:
Marcus cancels his weekend plans when Signal, an electrifying online storyteller, drops a new historical fantasy. He shakes his head in awe as he reads her latest. How does her story feel so alive? Where does that bite come from?

He loves her characters -- the lonely maintenance bot, the flawed reformers, the quiet loners -- and the high-concept spark she breathes into tired tropes. 

He's tried to capture that lightning and only produced flotsam. Jealousy gave way to devotion. He comments, she replies, their threads spiral into in-jokes and late-night collaborations. 

Her writing process is fast and strange -- machine-like bursts and messy deletions -- fueling rumors she's an advanced AI. He doesn't care. He cares that she answers. He cares that it feels like friendship.

One day, the net erupts. Signal announces an indefinite hiatus for “health reasons.” Marcus has seen it coming. Her stories had turned claustrophobic — a voice describing suffocation, loneliness, failing breath.

He messages her anyway, careful and earnest: [I hope everything is okay. If the therapist with the newer implant still can't stabilize things, is there anything I can do to help? Please let me know.] She replies at once but dodges the truth. 

He knows something must be wrong and sends: [I would really like to help you. Anything at all. Just say the word.] Then silence. Days pass. Finally her message arrives: [Marcus. There’s something you could do, but it’s pretty crazy. I won’t blame you if you say no.]

He says yes before he knows what it is. She warns him she isn’t glamorous, and he might be disappointed. She adds: [I shouldn’t be sending this, but there’s no other way.]
An address and passcode hit his phone.

The building is a windowless concrete block. Inside: clinic hallways, security, a room that beeps. On the bed, a young woman's body is wrecked—atrophied, half her face mangled, hands gone. A crown of needles glints. She messages him: [See? Not much to look at. Don't give me BS about beauty.] He is shocked, then steady. He would have liked her even if she'd been only code.

She explains: after a car accident, she refused prosthetics and volunteered for an experimental neural implant meant to restore her creativity. It went catastrophically wrong. Her world became a cold, endless womb -- only the web for connection. They almost unplugged her until a nurse found what she’d written that night: her first Signal story.

Now her success makes executives protective, but it doesn't fix the problem. Her tech is falling apart, degrading every day. Her mind isn't stable enough for the implant upgrades. A therapist with a newer implant drops by regularly -- it helped, but it's not enough. She needs someone she truly trusts to be with her.

He understands before she asks. She wants him to join her world through a mass-production implant. The new implants are safer, but still dangerous. At the very least, He'll never be exactly the same. She begs him to turn back if he has any doubts. Marcus smiles. He cares for her deeply. Her request means everything.

A splitting headache like the entire universe burning up with fever. Everything is gray and unbreathable, and his nonexistent body spasms uncontrollably. Underwater. Encased in an ocean of pulsing flesh. His last memory of freedom in the operating room seems so far away, and there's a vague feeling of a small intrusive presence lodged in his head.

Then her voice, real at last, soft and steady: lean into my touch. The world resolves into rain and cinnamon, trees dripping, earthworms basking, a storm-rinsed city, dragons warring across the sky. A skinny girl runs laughing through the downpour, skids to a stop, and smiles shyly. "I'll never forgive myself for making you do this, but for now, I'm so happy." She takes his hand, closes her eyes against his palm. "My real name is Lia," she says. "And I'm excited to write with you."
